title: Modi's First Ethiopia Visit: India Courts Global South Leadership Through African Engagement
lede: Prime Minister Narendra Modi arrived in Addis Ababa on December 16, 2025, for his first bilateral visit to Ethiopia, signaling New Delhi's strategic commitment to strengthening partnerships within the Global South.

sections.context: Prime Minister Modi accepted an invitation from Ethiopian Prime Minister Dr. Abiy Ahmed Ali for this inaugural bilateral visit to the Federal Democratic Republic of Ethiopia. According to India's Ministry of External Affairs, Ethiopia represents a particularly important partner for India within the Global South constellation of nations. The timing and framing of this visit underscore New Delhi's deliberate focus on deepening engagement with key African actors, positioning Ethiopia—home to the African Union headquarters and a regional power—as a critical node in India's broader Global South strategy. The visit's emphasis on strengthening bilateral partnership 'not just for ourselves, but also for the larger community in the Global South' reveals India's intent to use bilateral engagement as a platform for advancing collective Global South interests.
sections.threads: This visit connects to India's wider pattern of elevating engagement with Global South leaders and institutions. [[india-et-modis-first-ethiopia-visit-india-deepens-global-south-p-2026|Modi's recent Ethiopia engagement]] reflects a strategic pivot toward Africa as a theater for competing influence and building coalitions on issues of shared concern to developing nations. The visit also parallels India's simultaneous recalibration of partnerships across multiple regions—from elevated ties with France and South Korea to strengthened engagement with Myanmar—all designed to construct a coalition of Global South voices capable of advancing New Delhi's international priorities. Ethiopia's role as the seat of the African Union adds institutional weight to what might otherwise be a bilateral exercise, suggesting India views this engagement as foundational to shaping multilateral outcomes in African and Global South forums.
sections.signal: Analysts should monitor the substantive outcomes of Modi's Ethiopia visit for signals about the depth of India's Global South strategy. Key watch points include: the nature and scope of bilateral agreements signed during the visit; whether India establishes new development partnerships or defense cooperation frameworks with Ethiopia; and how prominently the two nations position their engagement within African Union mechanisms. Over the next 6-18 months, track whether this visit catalyzes a broader Indian diplomatic surge across East Africa or marks a one-off statement of intent. The real test will be whether India follows bilateral commitments with sustained institutional engagement—whether through trade promotion, development finance, or security partnerships—or whether the visit remains primarily symbolic. Additionally, monitor how Ethiopia positions this Indian engagement relative to its existing partnerships with other Global South actors and traditional partners.
